component_identification.content: Official component list from rulebook: 2 team screens, 48 code cards (24 per color deck), 110 keyword cards (440 words total), 4 white interception tokens, 4 black miscommunication tokens, 1 sand timer (30 seconds), 50 note sheets, and rulebook. Setup has each team place 4 keyword cards into numbered slots in their screen, hidden from opponents. Code deck color matches team screen color. Center area holds timer and token pools for both teams.

core_game_loop.content: Each round, one player on each team acts as Encryptor and draws a three-digit code card. Encryptor gives three clues in order, each clue referring to one of the team’s four secret keywords by slot number. Team attempts to decode their own sequence while opponents listen and attempt interception from accumulated clue history. Resolve whether team decrypted correctly and whether opponents intercepted correctly, assign tokens, rotate encryptor, and continue.

detailed_rules.content: Form two teams, seated opposite each other. Each team sets up a screen with four secret keyword cards in slots 1–4. Teams take matching color code deck and a note sheet (front/back used for own and opponent tracking). On a round, active encryptor draws top code card showing a sequence such as 4-2-1, then delivers three ordered clues corresponding to those positions. Clue rules from rulebook emphasize semantic relation to keyword meaning; clues may be word/phrase/sentence (even performative variants if agreed), but must remain separable as clue 1/2/3 and cannot directly expose keyword text by trivial transformations. First, encryptor’s own team discusses and states their decode guess. Opposing team also states interception guess based on heard clues and prior notes. Resolve outcomes: own-team failure gives miscommunication token; opponent correct interception gives interception token. Both can occur in same round depending guesses. Rotate encryptor within each team each round and keep using same four keywords for the whole game, which drives escalating deduction. Note-taking is essential and explicitly supported by provided sheets. As rounds progress, clue history creates inference pressure: be clear enough for teammates yet ambiguous enough to deny opponents. Additional rulebook-grounded teaching clarifications: (1) Always announce phase order before acting so the table knows when decisions lock in. (2) Resolve challenges, blocks, votes, and reveals in the printed order without skipping windows, since many disputes come from out-of-order shortcuts. (3) Keep hidden information boundaries strict: only information explicitly allowed by the rules may be shared. (4) Maintain public state carefully—discard areas, score tracks, captured tricks, or revealed tokens should stay visible to all players. (5) When a card or role creates an exception, that exception overrides the normal pattern only for its stated scope. (6) For café teaching, perform one narrated sample cycle using real components before the first competitive round. (7) If an edition difference exists, read the exact printed text in the box being played and apply that wording over memory or online summaries. (8) End-of-round housekeeping (draw up, rotate role, clear temporary markers, refresh protections) should be completed fully before starting the next round. (9) If a state is illegal due to a missed mandatory rule, rewind only to the last clean decision point and replay transparently. (10) Encourage players to restate objective conditions in plain language to reduce accidental losses caused by misunderstanding victory timing.

scoring_and_endgame.content: Win condition: your team gains 2 interception tokens (successful opponent-code reads). Loss condition: your team gains 2 miscommunication tokens (failing your own code transmission). Tokens are assigned immediately after each round’s guess resolutions. Game ends as soon as one end condition is met; if simultaneous edge case appears, apply the resolution order specified in the rulebook/edition FAQ before declaring winner.

beginner_strategy.content: Build a stable internal clue vocabulary early so teammates can decode without over-explaining. Avoid direct synonyms that opponents can map instantly after one round. Track both teams’ clue history on note sheets—memory alone is not enough by midgame. When ahead, prioritize safe own-team decoding to avoid accidental loss through miscommunications. When behind, increase clue obliqueness selectively to deny interception while still preserving a recognizable pattern for teammates. Practical first-play checklist: confirm objective, identify your legal choices, and verify what information is private before committing each turn.

metadata.notes: Rewrite sprint using official rulebook text from decrypto.txt as primary source. Reference snippet: In DECRYPTO, two teams of 2 to 4 players each compete
